profile.blurb: The ICU Medical Plum 360 is a large‑volume infusion pump widely used for accurate fluid delivery. Clinical engineers frequently encounter occlusion alarms, power management concerns, and configuration issues in the field.

commonIssues: Persistent occlusion alarms even when line appears clear — often due to incorrect cassette seating, roller guide issues, or UI misinterpreting pressure.
Pump won’t power on or unexpectedly shuts down — typically related to battery failure, faulty power adapter, or internal power board issues.
Inaccurate flow or delivery errors — caused by incorrect programming, worn cassette sensors, or calibration drift.
Error codes related to door/latch not closed — usually from debris on latch sensor or misaligned door.
Comm port or network errors on integrated models — cabling or configuration problems interfering with connectivity.
tips: Always verify correct cassette installation and ensure rollers and guides are clean before dismissing occlusion alarms.
Keep spare batteries charged and exercise them on a schedule to confirm capacity and prevent unexpected shutdowns.
Document and verify programming parameters with clinical staff to avoid delivery rate or volume mistakes.
Periodically clean and inspect the door/latch sensors to reduce false ‘door open’ or related errors.
When encountering network/comm errors, check physical connections first and use known‑good cables before advanced troubleshooting.
warnings: Do not force the pump door closed if resistance is felt — inspect for obstructions to avoid damaging sensors or latches.
Using third‑party or damaged power adapters can cause unstable performance or damage to internal electronics.
Ignoring occlusion alarms can lead to incorrect dosing; always investigate alarm cause before bypassing.

No breath or apnea-related alarms caused by true patient condition, disconnected sampling line, poor cannula placement, weak waveform, or low sample quality.
SpO2 sensor errors, sensor off patient alarms, or missing pulse readings caused by poor sensor placement, cable damage, patient movement, low perfusion, or incompatible accessories.
Battery low or unexpected shutdown complaints caused by depleted battery charge, AC power issues, aging battery pack, or damaged power connection.
False or nuisance alarms caused by incorrect alarm limits, patient movement, poor signal quality, improper accessory setup, or clinical condition changes.
tips: Always verify patient status and monitoring continuity before treating respiratory or pulse oximetry alarms as equipment problems.
Check the sampling line, cannula, filter, and connection path before suspecting an internal CO2 failure.
Use known-good approved sensors, cables, and sampling lines when isolating intermittent SpO2 or CO2 complaints.
Confirm alarm limits, waveform quality, numeric readings, and when the alarm occurs before documenting the issue.
For recurring battery complaints, test operation on AC power and battery power separately and inspect the power supply connection.
warnings: Do not dismiss EtCO2, respiration rate, SpO2, pulse rate, or no-breath alarms without clinical assessment of the patient.
Remove the monitor from patient use if CO2 or SpO2 functions are failed and those parameters are required for active monitoring.
Use only approved accessories and follow facility policy and manufacturer guidance when performing calibration, service, or safety testing.
